message.content:
## Adversarial Claim Verifier (voter 1/3)

Be SKEPTICAL. Try to REFUTE this claim. ≥2/3 refutations kill it.

## Research question
DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant.

## Claim under review
"Recognition scores for time-compressed (TC) speech systematically over-estimate recognition of naturally-produced fast-rate speech; listeners do substantially worse on natural fast speech than on time-compressed speech at a comparable rate, so a TC voice does not behave like genuinely fast speech."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6377055/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Recognition of time-compressed sentences over-estimated recognition of natural fast sentences for both groups, especially for older listeners."

## Checklist
1. Is the claim actually supported by the quote, or is it an overreach/misread?
2. WebSearch for contradicting evidence — does any credible source dispute or heavily qualify this?
3. Is the source quality sufficient for the claim's strength? (extraordinary claims need primary sources)
4. Is the claim outdated? (check dates — old claims about fast-moving fields are suspect)
5. Is this a marketing claim / press release / cherry-picked benchmark / forum speculation?

**refuted=true** if: unsupported by quote / contradicted / low-quality source for strong claim / outdated / marketing fluff.
**refuted=false** ONLY if: claim is well-supported, current, and source quality matches claim strength.
Default to refuted=true if uncertain.

Structured output only. Evidence MUST be specific.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this claim by examining the source and searching for corroborating/contradicting evidence.

input.prompt: What does this study say about recognition of time-compressed speech versus natural fast speech? Does time-compressed speech recognition over-estimate recognition of natural fast speech? Quote the specific findings, methods (how natural fast speech and time-compressed speech were generated and matched for rate), and the magnitude of any differences. Who were the listener groups?

input.query: time-compressed speech overestimates recognition natural fast speech intelligibility

content.content:
Web search results for query: "time-compressed speech overestimates recognition natural fast speech intelligibility"

Links: [{"title":"Intelligibility of time-compressed synthetic speech: Compression method and speaking style | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/282424493_Intelligibility_of_time-compressed_synthetic_speech_Compression_method_and_speaking_style"},{"title":"Techniques, Perception, and Applications of Time-Compressed Speech","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/2885840_Techniques_Perception_and_Applications_of_Time-Compressed_Speech"},{"title":"(PDF) Word-level intelligibility of time-compressed speech: Prosodic and segmental factors","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/222395525_Word-level_intelligibility_of_time-compressed_speech_Prosodic_and_segmental_factors"},{"title":"(PDF) Intelligibility analysis of fast synthesized speech","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/263487249_Intelligibility_analysis_of_fast_synthesized_speech"},{"title":"Intelligibility of time-compressed speech: the effect of uniform versus non-uniform time-compression algorithms - PubMed","url":"https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24606289/"},{"title":"Intelligibility of time-compressed synthetic speech: Compression method and speaking style - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639315000977"},{"title":"The Intelligibility of Time-Compressed Speech Is Correlated with the Ability to Listen in Modulated Noise - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9085996/"},{"title":"Time-compressed speech intelligibility in different reverberant conditions - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0003682X16301578"}]

Based on the search results, I found relevant research on time-compressed speech and intelligibility:

Researchers investigated whether making the temporal organization of artificially time-compressed speech more like that of natural fast speech would improve intelligibility over linear time compression. However, the findings revealed an important limitation: At very fast speech rates, applying fast speech timing worsens intelligibility, and this non-uniform way of speeding up may result from speakers' inability to speed up otherwise rather than from an underlying communicative principle.

Word recognition results for English voices show that generating speech at normal speaking rate and then applying linear compression resulted in the most intelligible speech at all tested rates. This suggests that linear compression applied to normal rate speech can more reliably provide higher intelligibility, particularly at ultra fast rates.

The research indicates that while mimicking natural fast speech patterns seems intuitively beneficial, in practice it does not outperform simpler linear compression methods for maintaining intelligibility, especially at extremely fast speech rates.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

# Study Findings on Time-Compressed vs. Natural Fast Speech

## Main Conclusion

Yes, the research confirms that time-compressed (TC) speech recognition substantially overestimates recognition of natural fast-rate speech. As the authors state: "Recognition of time-compressed sentences over-estimated recognition of natural fast sentences for both groups, especially for older listeners."

## Listener Groups

The study included:
- **13 younger normal-hearing (YNH) adults**: ages 19-22 (mean 20.4 years)
- **12 older normal-hearing (ONH) adults**: ages 66-76 (mean 68.8 years)

## Methods for Speech Generation

**Natural fast speech**: A talker produced sentences at a natural, conversational rate, then recorded them again at a naturally fast pace (targeting 50% time-compression but averaging 40%).

**Time-compressed speech**: The PSOLA algorithm in Praat applied 40% time-compression to normal-rate recordings to match the duration of naturally fast sentences.

## Magnitude of Differences

The performance gap was substantial, particularly for older listeners. In quiet conditions with low-context sentences, natural fast speech yielded significantly poorer recognition than TC speech. The authors note: "nearly all listeners exhibited higher recognition scores for 40TC speech materials than for natural fast rate speech" across all tested conditions (quiet and noise, both high and low-context sentences).

content.text: The source directly supports the claim and the supporting quote is accurate. Critically, the broader literature (the WebSearch result, including the Janse 2004 non-uniform compression study) does NOT contradict it — it reinforces it: linear time compression of normal-rate speech is MORE intelligible than mimicking natural fast-speech timing. Let me verify the direction is unambiguous and check whether any nuance qualifies the strength.

input.prompt: Confirm the exact title, authors, journal, and year of this article. Was the difference between time-compressed and natural fast speech statistically significant? Were there any conditions where natural fast speech was EQUAL to or BETTER than time-compressed speech? What acoustic differences (vowel reduction, consonant reduction, spectral differences) did they attribute the gap to?

content.content:
# Article Confirmation & Key Findings

**Title, Authors, Journal, Year:**
"Recognition of time-compressed speech does not predict recognition of natural fast-rate speech by older listeners" by Sandra Gordon-Salant, Danielle J Zion, and Carol Espy-Wilson, published in *The Journal of the Acoustical Society of America*, 2014.

**Statistical Significance:**
Yes, the difference was highly significant. The researchers found "significantly poorer recognition scores for natural fast-rate speech than for TC speech" across both quiet and noisy conditions (p < 0.001).

**Equal or Better Performance with Natural Fast Speech:**
No. The data showed that "all individual data points except one appear above the linear prediction line...indicating that recognition of TC materials was consistently higher than recognition of natural-fast speech materials" for both listener groups across all conditions.

**Acoustic Differences Identified:**
The authors attributed the gap to segmental distortions in natural fast speech. Their preliminary acoustic analyses revealed "not only shorter overall phoneme durations, but also deletions and distortions, especially of stops and affricates" caused by "co-articulation, which produces overlap of speech segments, and lenition, in which there is undershoot in reaching the target phoneme."

content.text:
The claim is well-supported on every checklist dimension. Let me record the verdict.

The claim is verified:

1. **Supported by quote**: The supporting quote ("Recognition of time-compressed sentences over-estimated recognition of natural fast sentences for both groups, especially for older listeners") directly states what the claim asserts. The claim is a faithful paraphrase, not an overreach. The article title itself is "Recognition of time-compressed speech does **not** predict recognition of natural fast-rate speech."

2. **No credible contradiction**: WebSearch surfaced corroborating literature (Janse 2004 on non-uniform compression; ScienceDirect work showing "generating speech at normal speaking rate and then applying linear compression resulted in the most intelligible speech at all tested rates"). This reinforces rather than refutes the claim — natural/mimicked fast timing is worse than linear TC, especially at ultra-fast rates.

3. **Source quality**: Primary peer-reviewed source — *Journal of the Acoustical Society of America* (Gordon-Salant, Zion, Espy-Wilson, 2014), a top-tier psychoacoustics journal. Matches the claim's strength.

4. **Not outdated**: 2014 study on stable psychoacoustic/phonetic phenomena (coarticulation, lenition, segmental undershoot in natural fast speech). This is not a fast-moving-tech claim; the underlying speech-production science is durable.

5. **Not marketing/forum/cherry-pick**: Controlled lab study, n=25 (13 YNH + 12 ONH), PSOLA-matched rates (40% compression matched to natural-fast duration), p<0.001, with all-but-one data points above the prediction line and a mechanistic acoustic explanation (deletions/distortions of stops and affricates, vowel undershoot).

One mild qualifier: the strongest effect was for *older* listeners, and the study used sentence recognition in quiet/noise — but the direction held for younger listeners and all conditions too, so the claim's generality is not an overreach.

input.evidence: The claim is faithfully supported by the primary source: Gordon-Salant, Zion & Espy-Wilson (2014), "Recognition of time-compressed speech does not predict recognition of natural fast-rate speech by older listeners," J. Acoust. Soc. Am. (PMC6377055). The supporting quote is accurate and the article title itself asserts TC does not predict natural-fast recognition. Method: a talker recorded sentences at natural-fast rate (~40% faster); PSOLA in Praat time-compressed normal-rate recordings to match that duration. Result: recognition was significantly poorer for natural fast speech than for rate-matched TC speech (p<0.001) across quiet and noise, high- and low-context sentences; for both younger (n=13, 19-22yo) and older (n=12, 66-76yo) listeners, ALL individual data points except one sat above the linear-prediction line, i.e., TC consistently overestimated natural-fast recognition (effect larger for older listeners). Mechanism attributed to segmental distortions in natural fast speech: shorter phoneme durations plus deletions/distortions of stops and affricates from coarticulation and lenition (target undershoot) — distortions linear TC does not introduce. Independent corroboration from WebSearch (Janse 2004 non-uniform vs uniform compression; ScienceDirect work) shows generating/mimicking natural fast timing is LESS intelligible than linear compression of normal-rate speech, especially at ultra-fast rates — reinforcing, not refuting. Source is primary, peer-reviewed, top-tier (JASA), and the underlying speech-production science is durable (not a fast-moving-tech claim). No credible contradicting source found.

content.text:
I have verified the claim and submitted my structured output. The claim is **not refuted** (refuted=false) with **high confidence**.

The claim accurately reflects the primary peer-reviewed source (Gordon-Salant et al., 2014, JASA), is statistically robust (p<0.001, effect held across both listener groups and all conditions), has a clear mechanistic explanation (coarticulation/lenition distortions in natural fast speech that linear time-compression does not introduce), and is independently corroborated rather than contradicted by adjacent literature.
